name: Tiki-Taka Passing Machine
strengths: Elite ball retention strangles opponents out of rhythm
Defensive structure inherits from possession
Composed press triggers in midfield turnovers
cautions: Vulnerable to vertical counter-pressing
Can stall against a disciplined deep block
Cycle-dependent on a generational midfield core
summary: Possession-heavy sides that build through short passing, positional rotations, and patient midfield control. Wins through suffocation, not transition.

name: Counter-Attacking Specialist
strengths: Lethal transition speed turns one chance into a goal
Compact mid-block forces opponents into mistakes
Two reliable forwards capable of running 60 metres
cautions: Struggles when forced to break down a packed defence
Tournament heat reduces sprint capacity by week three
One injury to the outlet striker collapses the model
summary: Pragmatic sides that cede possession, absorb pressure, and strike on the break. Tournament football's perennially underrated archetype.

name: High-Press Aggressor
strengths: Wins the ball 35 metres from goal — shortest distance to score
Forces top-tier opponents into low-quality clearances
Compresses the pitch and dictates pace
cautions: Press resistance is the cheat code that breaks it
Massively fitness-dependent — fades in extra time
Set-pieces conceded behind the line become open chances
summary: Front-foot sides that hunt the ball in the opposition's half, compress space, and convert turnovers into shots. Defines modern tournament football.

name: Defensive Catenaccio
strengths: Concedes the fewest expected goals in the tournament
Comfortable defending one-goal leads for 80 minutes
Goalkeeper and centre-back pairing is best-in-class
cautions: Must convert limited chances — striker form is mission-critical
Modern referees punish the tactical foul tax
Penalty shootouts become the realistic endgame
summary: Defence-first sides that strangle games to 0-0 and decide tournaments with one set-piece or one moment of individual brilliance. Old-school but enduring.

name: Total Football
strengths: Every outfielder is comfortable in every zone
Positional rotations dissolve traditional matchups
Aesthetic dominance often translates to belief
cautions: Famously trophy-poor relative to the talent it produces
Internal politics surface under tournament pressure
One disciplined opponent can break the rotation
summary: The beautiful-game blueprint: fluid, rotational, technically gifted sides where every player attacks and every player defends. Often runners-up — rarely champions.

name: Samba Flair
strengths: Two or three individual moments win knockout games
Front-line creativity untouched by tactical structure
Brand and aura intimidate group-stage opponents
cautions: Defensive shape can crack against organised European sides
Star culture risks fragmenting a dressing room
Heat-dependent — Brazil-style flair fades in cooler climates
summary: Star-led sides leaning on virtuoso individual moments — dribbles, free-kicks, audacious finishes. The original tournament cheat code when the front line is on form.

name: Physical Long-Ball
strengths: Set-piece and second-ball superiority
Hard to outwork in physical duels
Limited build-up reduces turnover risk in own half
cautions: Modern technical sides exploit the long-ball lottery
Caps the ceiling — rarely beats elite midfields twice
Yellow-card management becomes critical in knockouts
summary: Direct, physical sides that skip the midfield, attack set-pieces, and out-muscle opponents. Tournament dark-horse template — high floor, low ceiling.

name: Dark-Horse Spoiler
strengths: Tactical flexibility — can switch shapes mid-tournament
Underrated technically and emotionally over-prepared
Goalkeeper and set-pieces punch well above weight
cautions: One knockout slip ends the entire run
Squad depth thins quickly through cards and fatigue
Spotlight pressure intensifies after the first upset
summary: Mid-tier nations that catch fire — well-drilled defensively, lethal on transitions, and powered by belief. The bracket-buster archetype.

name: Tactical Pragmatists
strengths: Shape and intensity dialled to the opponent each match
Elite individual talent across all four lines
Comfortable winning ugly, winning pretty, winning on penalties
cautions: Risks looking passive in group stage and falling behind early
Star-management is a season-long project
Tournament identity is sometimes opaque to its own fans
summary: Mode-shifting sides with elite talent and no tactical ego — they play to the opponent, the scoreline, and the temperature. Modern champions look like this.

name: Mid-Block Disciplinarians
strengths: Defensive shape rarely breaks under tournament pressure
Mid-block triggers reliable counter springs
Squad culture is process-driven, low-drama
cautions: Lacks an A+ knockout-stage difference-maker
Tactical fouls add up in two-legged scenarios
Can be over-coached against tournament outliers
summary: Compact, drilled, disciplined sides that defend in a tight mid-block and counter through their best two attackers. Reliable to the semis, rarely past them.

name: Wing-Play Dynamos
strengths: Wide overloads punish narrow defensive blocks
Cross-and-finish chains generate consistent xG
Two-footed wingers are nightmare 1v1 matchups
cautions: Striker dependency — empty-net finishers required
Sides defending the byline neutralise the threat
Fullback workload spikes injury risk by week three
summary: Width-first sides that overload the flanks, stretch back-fours, and feed a clinical box-finisher. Classic World Cup blueprint when the wide players are world-class.

name: Rebuilding Generation
strengths: Upside scenario is genuinely uncapped
Squad chemistry has time to compound for 2030
Less tactical baggage — willing to try shape changes
cautions: Inexperience visible in tournament moments
Coach-player trust still being built
Group of Death exposes the depth chart fast
summary: Squads mid-transition — new spine, new manager, generational handover. Tournament is a learning lap; the real campaign is four years away.
